Executing the worst offenders is not about bloodlust. It is about refusing to tell grieving families that their loss does not warrant society's most serious response.

## Deterrence: The Lives Saved by Credible Punishment

Beyond its retributive justification, capital punishment serves a forward-looking purpose: it saves innocent lives by deterring future murders. This claim, long dismissed in some academic circles, has been revived by a wave of serious econometric research. Economists Hashem Dezhbakhsh, Paul Rubin, and Joanna Shepherd at Emory University, using county-level panel data and instrumental variables, concluded that each execution in the United States is associated with the prevention of approximately eighteen murders. Their work, along with studies by Naci Mocan and R. Kaj Gittings at the University of Colorado and Paul Zimmerman at the FTC, consistently finds measurable deterrent effects—not only from executions themselves but from death sentences and shorter waits on death row.

The intuition behind this evidence is straightforward. Deterrence theory, which underlies nearly every domain of criminal law, assumes that rational actors respond to incentives at the margin. We accept this premise when we increase penalties for drunk driving, wire fraud, or tax evasion. Only in the capital context do critics suddenly insist that would-be offenders are immune to incentives. That position is implausible on its face. A murderer contemplating whether to kill a witness, a gang enforcer weighing the consequences of an execution-style hit, a kidnapper deciding whether to leave his victim alive—these are precisely the calculations in which the credible threat of death changes behavior. Even if the deterrent effect is smaller than the Emory studies suggest, the moral arithmetic is stark: if executing the worst murderers saves even a handful of innocent lives per execution, opposition to the death penalty requires valuing the life of the convicted killer above the lives of his future victims.

Critics often point to the low national homicide rate in European countries without the death penalty. But this is a confusion of correlation and causation; those societies differ from the United States in countless ways unrelated to sentencing. The relevant question is not whether Norway has less murder than Texas, but whether the same American jurisdiction, holding other factors constant, experiences fewer murders when capital punishment is credibly enforced. The serious empirical literature increasingly answers yes.

## An Indispensable Prosecutorial Tool

Capital punishment's value extends beyond sentencing into the investigative and prosecutorial stages, where it functions as perhaps the most powerful tool American prosecutors possess. The credible threat of a death sentence induces guilty pleas, unlocks cooperation against co-conspirators, and produces confessions that solve cold cases. Remove that threat, and countless investigations stall at precisely the moment they need to break open.

Consider Gary Ridgway, the Green River Killer. For two decades, the families of dozens of murdered women in Washington State waited for answers. When Ridgway was finally charged, it was the prospect of execution that moved him to plead guilty and lead investigators to the remains of victims whose fates would otherwise have remained unknown. His plea deal—life imprisonment in exchange for full cooperation—solved forty-nine murders and gave closure to families who had spent decades in anguish. Without the leverage of capital punishment, there was nothing to trade. Similar dynamics produced cooperation from figures like Terry Nichols in the Oklahoma City bombing investigation and countless lower-profile cases in which the threat of the ultimate sanction has cracked conspiracies, identified accomplices, and located bodies.

This prosecutorial function matters for a reason rarely acknowledged by abolitionists: the death penalty, by its very existence, makes life imprisonment possible as a negotiated outcome. When the maximum sentence is already life without parole, prosecutors have nothing left to offer a hardened killer in exchange for cooperation. The defendant who murdered three victims has no reason to disclose the fourth. The terrorist has no reason to identify his handlers. The gang leader has no reason to testify against the cartel above him. The death penalty is the lever that moves these cases; without it, the lever is gone, and justice for countless secondary victims goes with it.

Families of the missing and the unsolved have a stake in this debate that is seldom heard. For them, the abstract question of whether the state should ever execute a murderer is inseparable from the concrete question of whether their daughter's killer will ever be identified, whether their son's remains will ever be recovered, whether they will live the rest of their lives without answers. Capital punishment, used judiciously, is what produces those answers.

## Moral Seriousness and the Protection of Society

A society that refuses to execute its worst monsters is making a statement about what it values and what it will tolerate. When Norway sentenced Anders Breivik—a man who murdered 77 people, most of them teenagers—to a maximum of 21 years in a cell with a television, video games, and exercise equipment, it was not demonstrating moral sophistication. It was demonstrating that even mass murder carries a ceiling of consequence more appropriate to serious fraud. That is moral unseriousness dressed up as enlightenment.

Capital punishment also offers a form of protection that life imprisonment cannot guarantee. Incarcerated killers have murdered guards and fellow inmates. Some have escaped. Some have been released through sentence commutations, appellate reversals, or the drift of future policy. An executed murderer commits no further crimes. For figures like Richard Ramirez, the "Night Stalker," or Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, the promise that they will never again touch an innocent life is not a small thing—it is the state fulfilling its most fundamental duty to protect its citizens.

## Addressing the Strongest Objection

The most serious objection to capital punishment is the risk of executing an innocent person. This concern deserves respect, not dismissal. But the answer is not abolition—it is rigor. Modern DNA evidence, video surveillance, expanded discovery rules, and mandatory appellate review mean that in cases where guilt is overwhelming and uncontested, the chance of wrongful execution approaches zero. When a terrorist is captured on camera detonating a bomb, when a killer confesses and leads police to bodies, when DNA conclusively ties a serial predator to his victims—the epistemic uncertainty that rightly troubles us in ambiguous cases simply does not apply. Reserving capital punishment for cases of overwhelming, uncontested guilt addresses the innocence concern while preserving justice for the clearest monsters.

## Conclusion

A civilization is defined not only by its mercy but by its judgments. To say that nothing a human being does can warrant execution is not to affirm the value of life—it is to deny that some acts truly desecrate it. The case for capital punishment rests on four mutually reinforcing pillars: it delivers proportionate justice to victims and their families; it deters future murders and thereby saves innocent lives; it provides prosecutors with the indispensable leverage needed to solve cold cases and dismantle criminal conspiracies; and it permanently incapacitates the worst offenders among us. The United States should stop treating capital punishment as an embarrassment to be minimized and start treating it as what it is: a grave, deliberate, and morally necessary response to the small number of human beings who commit acts of calculated horror against the innocent. For the serial killer, the child murderer, the mass-casualty terrorist, justice requires more than a cell. It requires an answer proportionate to what they have done. America should have the moral clarity to deliver it.

When a serial killer tortures victims across decades, when a terrorist slaughters civilians to advance an ideology, when a predator rapes and murders a child—society faces a moral choice. It can respond with proportionate justice, or it can flinch. The United States should stop flinching. Capital punishment is not a relic to be quietly retired; it is a necessary instrument of justice that America should actively employ against the worst offenders among us.

## Proportionality Is the Foundation of Justice

The core principle of any legitimate justice system is that punishment must fit the crime. We do not fine murderers twenty dollars, and we do not imprison jaywalkers for life. Proportionality is not vengeance—it is the recognition that a society's response to wrongdoing must reflect the gravity of what was done. When Ted Bundy murdered dozens of women, when Timothy McVeigh killed 168 people including 19 children in Oklahoma City, when Dylann Roof executed nine parishioners mid-prayer at a Charleston church—these acts exist on a moral plane that demands more than room, board, and cable television for the rest of the perpetrator's natural life.

Life imprisonment, whatever its virtues, treats the annihilation of innocent human beings as commensurate with lesser violent crimes. It flattens the moral landscape. The death penalty, reserved for the worst of the worst, restores the distinction. It says: there is a category of evil so profound that the perpetrator has forfeited the most basic claim any person holds—the claim to continued existence within the human community.

## Justice for Victims, Not Just Mercy for Perpetrators

Modern debates about capital punishment have a curious tendency to center almost entirely on the murderer. What are his rights? What is his suffering? What if he has reformed? Less is said about the woman who was raped and strangled, the child whose last moments were filled with terror, the families who will carry that loss until they die.

A justice system that cannot deliver the ultimate sanction for the ultimate crime tells victims' families that their loved ones' lives were not worth an equivalent response. For the families of the Boston Marathon bombing victims, for the parents of the children murdered at Sandy Hook, for the survivors of the Pulse nightclub massacre—the message that the killer will live out his days, write memoirs, give interviews, and perhaps even find a pen pal romance is its own kind of cruelty. Executing the worst offenders is not about bloodlust. It is about refusing to tell grieving families that their loss does not warrant society's most serious response.

## Deterrence: The Lives Saved by Credible Punishment

Beyond its retributive justification, capital punishment serves a forward-looking purpose: it saves innocent lives by deterring future murders. This claim, long dismissed in some academic circles, has been revived by a wave of serious econometric research. Economists Hashem Dezhbakhsh, Paul Rubin, and Joanna Shepherd at Emory University, using county-level panel data and instrumental variables, concluded that each execution in the United States is associated with the prevention of approximately eighteen murders. Their work, along with studies by Naci Mocan and R. Kaj Gittings at the University of Colorado and Paul Zimmerman at the FTC, consistently finds measurable deterrent effects—not only from executions themselves but from death sentences and shorter waits on death row.

The intuition behind this evidence is straightforward. Deterrence theory, which underlies nearly every domain of criminal law, assumes that rational actors respond to incentives at the margin. We accept this premise when we increase penalties for drunk driving, wire fraud, or tax evasion. Only in the capital context do critics suddenly insist that would-be offenders are immune to incentives. That position is implausible on its face. A murderer contemplating whether to kill a witness, a gang enforcer weighing the consequences of an execution-style hit, a kidnapper deciding whether to leave his victim alive—these are precisely the calculations in which the credible threat of death changes behavior. Even if the deterrent effect is smaller than the Emory studies suggest, the moral arithmetic is stark: if executing the worst murderers saves even a handful of innocent lives per execution, opposition to the death penalty requires valuing the life of the convicted killer above the lives of his future victims.

Critics often point to the low national homicide rate in European countries without the death penalty. But this is a confusion of correlation and causation; those societies differ from the United States in countless ways unrelated to sentencing. The relevant question is not whether Norway has less murder than Texas, but whether the same American jurisdiction, holding other factors constant, experiences fewer murders when capital punishment is credibly enforced. The serious empirical literature increasingly answers yes.

## An Indispensable Prosecutorial Tool

Capital punishment's value extends beyond sentencing into the investigative and prosecutorial stages, where it functions as perhaps the most powerful tool American prosecutors possess. The credible threat of a death sentence induces guilty pleas, unlocks cooperation against co-conspirators, and produces confessions that solve cold cases. Remove that threat, and countless investigations stall at precisely the moment they need to break open.

Consider Gary Ridgway, the Green River Killer. For two decades, the families of dozens of murdered women in Washington State waited for answers. When Ridgway was finally charged, it was the prospect of execution that moved him to plead guilty and lead investigators to the remains of victims whose fates would otherwise have remained unknown. His plea deal—life imprisonment in exchange for full cooperation—solved forty-nine murders and gave closure to families who had spent decades in anguish. Without the leverage of capital punishment, there was nothing to trade. Similar dynamics produced cooperation from figures like Terry Nichols in the Oklahoma City bombing investigation and countless lower-profile cases in which the threat of the ultimate sanction has cracked conspiracies, identified accomplices, and located bodies.

This prosecutorial function matters for a reason rarely acknowledged by abolitionists: the death penalty, by its very existence, makes life imprisonment possible as a negotiated outcome. When the maximum sentence is already life without parole, prosecutors have nothing left to offer a hardened killer in exchange for cooperation. The defendant who murdered three victims has no reason to disclose the fourth. The terrorist has no reason to identify his handlers. The gang leader has no reason to testify against the cartel above him. The death penalty is the lever that moves these cases; without it, the lever is gone, and justice for countless secondary victims goes with it.

Families of the missing and the unsolved have a stake in this debate that is seldom heard. For them, the abstract question of whether the state should ever execute a murderer is inseparable from the concrete question of whether their daughter's killer will ever be identified, whether their son's remains will ever be recovered, whether they will live the rest of their lives without answers. Capital punishment, used judiciously, is what produces those answers.

## Constitutional Foundation and Democratic Legitimacy

Capital punishment is not a policy imposed on a reluctant public by overreaching lawmakers; it is a practice rooted in the Constitution itself and sustained by the considered judgment of the American people. The Fifth Amendment explicitly contemplates it, providing that no person shall "be deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law"—language that presupposes the state's authority to deprive a person of life when due process has been observed. The same amendment requires grand jury indictment for "a capital, or otherwise infamous crime." The Fourteenth Amendment repeats the formulation. The men who wrote and ratified these provisions did not regard execution as a constitutional anomaly; they wrote the death penalty into the very text that protects individual rights.

The Supreme Court has recognized this reality repeatedly. In Gregg v. Georgia (1976), the Court definitively upheld capital punishment against an Eighth Amendment challenge, observing that the death penalty "is an expression of society's moral outrage at particularly offensive conduct" and that retribution is neither "a forbidden objective" nor "inconsistent with our respect for the dignity of men." Gregg has been reaffirmed, refined, and applied in case after case for nearly half a century. Justices across the ideological spectrum have acknowledged that the Constitution permits capital punishment; the debate at the Court has been about procedural safeguards and categorical exemptions, not about the fundamental legitimacy of the practice.

Democratic legitimacy reinforces this constitutional foundation. Gallup has tracked American opinion on the death penalty for decades, and majority support has proven remarkably durable—hovering in the 53 to 55 percent range in recent years even as progressive advocacy groups have invested heavily in turning public opinion. When pollsters ask about specific categories of the worst offenders—mass shooters, terrorists, child murderers—support climbs dramatically higher, often into supermajority territory. In states where capital punishment has been put directly to voters, the results have been telling: California voters rejected abolition ballot measures in both 2012 and 2016, and in 2016 approved a separate measure designed to accelerate executions. Nebraska voters, in 2016, overturned their legislature's abolition of the death penalty by a substantial margin. When the question is put squarely to the American people, they have repeatedly and consistently answered that capital punishment should remain available for the worst crimes.

This is precisely why abolitionists have increasingly abandoned the democratic arena in favor of judicial maneuvering, executive clemency campaigns, and pharmaceutical pressure tactics designed to make executions logistically impossible. Unable to persuade majorities at the ballot box or in state legislatures, opponents have turned to litigation strategies seeking to have capital punishment declared categorically unconstitutional—despite the text of the Constitution, the weight of precedent, and the sustained judgment of the voting public. They have pressured European drug manufacturers to refuse sales to American corrections departments, hoping to achieve through supply-chain sabotage what they cannot achieve through argument. They have urged governors to empty death rows by executive fiat, as outgoing governors in several states have done over the objections of their own prosecutors and constituents.

These tactics are, at their core, anti-democratic. A policy embedded in the constitutional text, repeatedly upheld by the Supreme Court, supported by durable popular majorities, and enacted by the elected representatives of more than half the states is not a policy that a narrow elite is entitled to nullify through back-channel pressure. The democratic case for capital punishment does not depend on whether any individual reader finds it morally attractive. It depends on the principle that fundamental questions of criminal justice—questions about which reasonable citizens disagree—should be resolved through constitutional and democratic processes, not through the quiet efforts of activists who have concluded that their moral intuitions should override those of their fellow citizens. Americans have chosen capital punishment, and they have chosen it knowing full well what it entails. That choice is entitled to respect.

## Moral Seriousness and the Protection of Society

A society that refuses to execute its worst monsters is making a statement about what it values and what it will tolerate. When Norway sentenced Anders Breivik—a man who murdered 77 people, most of them teenagers—to a maximum of 21 years in a cell with a television, video games, and exercise equipment, it was not demonstrating moral sophistication. It was demonstrating that even mass murder carries a ceiling of consequence more appropriate to serious fraud. That is moral unseriousness dressed up as enlightenment.

Capital punishment also offers a form of protection that life imprisonment cannot guarantee. Incarcerated killers have murdered guards and fellow inmates. Some have escaped. Some have been released through sentence commutations, appellate reversals, or the drift of future policy. An executed murderer commits no further crimes. For figures like Richard Ramirez, the "Night Stalker," or Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, the promise that they will never again touch an innocent life is not a small thing—it is the state fulfilling its most fundamental duty to protect its citizens.

## Addressing the Strongest Objection

The most serious objection to capital punishment is the risk of executing an innocent person. This concern deserves respect, not dismissal. But the answer is not abolition—it is rigor. Modern DNA evidence, video surveillance, expanded discovery rules, and mandatory appellate review mean that in cases where guilt is overwhelming and uncontested, the chance of wrongful execution approaches zero. When a terrorist is captured on camera detonating a bomb, when a killer confesses and leads police to bodies, when DNA conclusively ties a serial predator to his victims—the epistemic uncertainty that rightly troubles us in ambiguous cases simply does not apply. Reserving capital punishment for cases of overwhelming, uncontested guilt addresses the innocence concern while preserving justice for the clearest monsters.

## Conclusion

A civilization is defined not only by its mercy but by its judgments. To say that nothing a human being does can warrant execution is not to affirm the value of life—it is to deny that some acts truly desecrate it. The case for capital punishment rests on five mutually reinforcing pillars: it delivers proportionate justice to victims and their families; it deters future murders and thereby saves innocent lives; it provides prosecutors with the indispensable leverage needed to solve cold cases and dismantle criminal conspiracies; it rests on firm constitutional ground and enduring democratic support; and it permanently incapacitates the worst offenders among us. The United States should stop treating capital punishment as an embarrassment to be minimized and start treating it as what it is: a grave, deliberate, and morally necessary response to the small number of human beings who commit acts of calculated horror against the innocent. For the serial killer, the child murderer, the mass-casualty terrorist, justice requires more than a cell. It requires an answer proportionate to what they have done. America should have the moral clarity to deliver it.

# Justice Demands the Death Penalty: Why America Must Actively Use Capital Punishment for the Worst Crimes

## Introduction: The Petit Family

On July 23, 2007, in Cheshire, Connecticut, two career criminals named Steven Hayes and Joshua Komisarjevsky invaded the home of Dr. William Petit. Over the course of seven hours, they beat Dr. Petit with a baseball bat, tied him in the basement, and subjected his wife Jennifer and their daughters Hayley, 17, and Michaela, 11, to an ordeal of sexual assault and terror. When they were finished, they strangled Jennifer, tied the girls to their beds, doused the house in gasoline, and set it on fire. Hayley and Michaela, still alive, died of smoke inhalation as they lay bound in their bedrooms. Dr. Petit, the sole survivor, escaped to a neighbor's yard.

Hayes and Komisarjevsky were sentenced to death. Neither has been executed. In 2012, Connecticut's legislature abolished capital punishment prospectively; in 2015, the state's Supreme Court extended that abolition retroactively by a 4-3 vote, sparing the men who murdered the Petit women and dozens of others on the state's death row. Today Hayes and Komisarjevsky are alive at taxpayer expense, housed, fed, and medically cared for, while Hayley and Michaela are not.

This is the reality American policy has drifted toward: a system in which the most calculated, sadistic, documented murderers of innocents continue to breathe while their victims do not, and in which the law's response to the extinction of human life is a cell and a meal tray. It is a moral inversion. It must end. The United States should actively, unapologetically use the death penalty for the worst crimes—and the case for doing so is not merely defensible but overwhelming, converging from multiple independent directions into a single unified argument.

## Proportionality: The Foundation on Which Everything Else Rests

The first and most fundamental argument is proportionality. Every legitimate justice system rests on the principle that punishment must reflect the gravity of the wrong. When Dennis Rader—the BTK killer—bound, tortured, and strangled ten people across decades in Kansas, including an eleven-year-old girl hanged in a basement while he photographed her; when Chester Turner raped and murdered at least fourteen women in Los Angeles and was later linked to more; when Timothy McVeigh killed 168 people, nineteen of them children in a daycare center—these acts exist on a moral plane that demands more than room, board, and cable television for the rest of the perpetrator's natural life.

Life imprisonment, whatever its virtues, treats the annihilation of innocent human beings as commensurate with lesser violent crimes. It flattens the moral landscape. The death penalty, reserved for the worst of the worst, restores the distinction. It says: there is a category of evil so profound that the perpetrator has forfeited the most basic claim any person holds—the claim to continued existence within the human community. This is not vengeance; it is the precondition for a justice system that takes human life seriously. And it is the foundation on which every subsequent argument builds.

## From Proportionate Justice to Lives Saved: The Deterrence Evidence

Proportionality is not only a retributive principle; it is also the mechanism by which criminal law shapes behavior. Because punishment must fit the crime, the gravest punishments attach to the gravest offenses—and those punishments deter. A wave of serious econometric research has confirmed what common sense has always suggested: credibly enforced capital punishment saves innocent lives.

Economists Hashem Dezhbakhsh, Paul Rubin, and Joanna Shepherd at Emory University, using county-level panel data and instrumental-variable techniques, concluded that each execution in the United States is associated with the prevention of approximately eighteen murders. Their findings have been reinforced by Naci Mocan and R. Kaj Gittings at Colorado, who found that not only executions but death sentences and shorter waits on death row produce measurable reductions in homicide; by Paul Zimmerman at the FTC; and by other scholars working with different datasets and methodologies. The deterrent effect is not a fringe hypothesis. It is a robust empirical finding reached by serious economists applying the standard tools of their discipline.

The intuition is straightforward. Deterrence theory underlies every domain of criminal law. We accept that it works for drunk driving, wire fraud, and tax evasion. Only in the capital context do critics suddenly insist that would-be offenders are immune to incentives—a position that is implausible on its face. A murderer weighing whether to kill a witness, a gang enforcer calculating the consequences of an execution-style hit, a kidnapper deciding whether to leave his victim alive: these are precisely the calculations in which the credible threat of death alters behavior.

The moral arithmetic is therefore stark. If executing the worst murderers saves even a fraction of the lives the Emory studies suggest, then opposition to capital punishment requires valuing the life of the convicted killer above the lives of his future victims. Proportionality tells us such a killer deserves to die. Deterrence tells us his death will spare others from the same fate. These are not competing arguments but complementary ones, pointing in the same direction from different angles.

## From Deterrence to Solved Cases: The Prosecutorial Lever

Deterrence operates on future crimes; the death penalty also operates on past ones. It is perhaps the most powerful tool American prosecutors possess—not merely as a sentence but as the leverage that unlocks guilty pleas, cooperation against co-conspirators, and confessions that solve cold cases.

Consider Gary Ridgway, the Green River Killer. For two decades, families of dozens of murdered women in Washington State waited for answers. When Ridgway was finally charged, it was the prospect of execution—and only the prospect of execution—that moved him to plead guilty and lead investigators to remains whose locations would otherwise have died with him. His cooperation resolved forty-nine murders. Without the threat of the ultimate sanction, there was nothing to trade, and forty-nine families would still be waiting. Terry Nichols cooperated in the Oklahoma City investigation under similar pressure. Countless lower-profile cases follow the same pattern: the death penalty is the lever that cracks conspiracies, identifies accomplices, and locates bodies.

Abolitionists rarely acknowledge a critical consequence of their position: when the maximum sentence is already life without parole, prosecutors have nothing left to offer a hardened killer in exchange for cooperation. The defendant who murdered three victims has no reason to disclose the fourth. The terrorist has no reason to identify his handlers. The gang leader has no reason to testify against the cartel above him. Abolish the death penalty and you do not merely spare the condemned; you silence them, and you consign their secondary victims—the missing, the unidentified, the buried—to permanent darkness.

The throughline grows clearer. Proportionality establishes what killers deserve. Deterrence shows that giving them what they deserve saves future innocents. Prosecutorial leverage shows that the mere availability of this sanction delivers justice to past innocents whose cases would otherwise remain unsolved. Each argument amplifies the others.

## From Justice and Prevention to Democratic Mandate

Capital punishment is not some elite project imposed on a reluctant public. It is rooted in the Constitution itself and sustained by the considered judgment of the American people. The Fifth Amendment explicitly contemplates it, providing that no person shall "be deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law"—language that presupposes the state's authority to deprive a person of life when due process has been observed. The same amendment requires grand jury indictment for "a capital, or otherwise infamous crime." The Fourteenth Amendment repeats the formulation. The Founders did not regard execution as a constitutional anomaly; they wrote it into the very text that protects individual rights.

The Supreme Court has recognized this reality repeatedly. In Gregg v. Georgia (1976), the Court upheld capital punishment against Eighth Amendment challenge, observing that the death penalty "is an expression of society's moral outrage at particularly offensive conduct" and that retribution is neither "a forbidden objective" nor "inconsistent with our respect for the dignity of men." Gregg has been reaffirmed, refined, and applied for nearly half a century.

Democratic legitimacy reinforces constitutional foundation. Gallup has tracked American opinion on the death penalty for decades, and majority support has proven remarkably durable—hovering in the 53 to 55 percent range even as progressive advocacy groups have poured resources into turning public opinion. When pollsters ask about mass shooters, terrorists, and child murderers specifically, support climbs into supermajority territory. Where capital punishment has been put directly to voters, the results have been unambiguous: California rejected abolition ballot measures in 2012 and 2016 and in the latter year approved a measure to accelerate executions; Nebraska voters overturned their legislature's abolition by substantial margin in 2016. Americans have chosen capital punishment, knowing full well what it entails.

This is precisely why abolitionists have increasingly abandoned the democratic arena for judicial maneuvering, executive clemency campaigns, and pharmaceutical pressure tactics designed to make executions logistically impossible. Unable to persuade majorities at the ballot box or in state legislatures, they have pressured European drug manufacturers to cut off supplies, urged lame-duck governors to empty death rows by fiat, and litigated endlessly to freeze executions under technical challenges. These are not arguments; they are workarounds. They are, at their core, anti-democratic—the efforts of a narrow elite determined to nullify through procedural sabotage what it cannot win through persuasion.

So the case now stands on four pillars. Proportionality establishes what justice requires. Deterrence shows that justice also saves lives. Prosecutorial leverage shows that justice also solves past crimes. And democratic legitimacy shows that the American people, speaking through their Constitution, their courts, their legislatures, and their ballot initiatives, have repeatedly endorsed exactly this system.

## The Innocence Myth: The Great Inversion

There remains the one argument abolitionists believe insulates them from every other consideration: the claim that the system executes innocents. It is their most powerful rhetorical weapon. It is also their most dishonest.

The claim rests almost entirely on the Death Penalty Information Center's so-called "innocence list," which currently catalogs roughly 190 "exonerations" since 1973. The number is dramatic. It is also profoundly misleading. DPIC counts as an "exoneration" any case in which a death-row conviction is vacated and the defendant is not subsequently retried and reconvicted for the capital offense—regardless of whether the reversal had anything to do with actual innocence. A conviction thrown out over a flawed jury instruction counts. A reversal based on a prosecutor's failure to turn over marginal impeachment evidence counts. A case dropped decades later because witnesses have died counts. A plea to a lesser offense counts. None of these categories requires any affirmative showing that the defendant did not commit the murder.

Former federal prosecutor and University of Utah law professor Paul Cassell and longtime California deputy attorney general Ward Campbell have examined DPIC's list in exhaustive detail. Their case-by-case analyses conclude that only a small fraction of DPIC's "exonerees" have any credible claim to factual innocence; the overwhelming majority won reversals on procedural grounds while the evidence of guilt remained intact. Many confessed. Many were implicated by co-defendants, physical evidence, or eyewitnesses whose testimony was never credibly recanted. They are not innocents plucked from the jaws of execution; they are guilty men who exploited procedural irregularities. Conflating the two is not journalism. It is propaganda.

The decisive question is this: in the modern era of capital punishment—since the 1976 restoration—can abolitionists identify a single person executed in the United States who has been proven, by clear and convincing evidence, to have been factually innocent of the crime for which he was put to death? They cannot. Despite decades of well-funded activism, despite the Innocence Project's aggressive post-conviction investigations, despite journalists combing death-row records for the story that would finally prove the system's deadly failure, no such case has been established. The handful of names routinely floated as candidates—Willingham, DeLuna, Cantu—are each contested, each depends on advocacy-driven reinterpretation of ambiguous evidence, and none meets the evidentiary standard required to overturn a conviction in any courtroom. The much-feared flood of executed innocents has not materialized, because the combination of trial safeguards, automatic appellate review, federal habeas corpus, DNA testing, and executive clemency has produced a system whose demonstrated error rate at the point of execution approaches zero.

Now contrast that near-zero risk with a category of harm abolitionists will not discuss: the innocents killed by murderers who were spared. Kenneth McDuff was sentenced to death in Texas in 1966 for murdering three teenagers; his sentence was commuted after Furman, and he was paroled in 1989. Over the next three years, he murdered at least nine more women before being recaptured. Arthur Shawcross served fifteen years for killing two children, was released, and proceeded to murder eleven women in Rochester. Clarence Ray Allen, serving life for murder, orchestrated additional killings from inside prison. Guards and inmates have been murdered by lifers who had nothing left to lose. These are not abstractions. They are named victims, with grieving families, whose deaths trace directly to the decision not to execute men who had already proven what they were.

This is the real moral calculus, and it inverts the abolitionist framing entirely. On one side: a theoretical, unverified, near-zero risk that an innocent person might be executed under extensive procedural safeguards. On the other: a documented, recurring, non-zero toll of innocents murdered by killers who were not executed when they should have been. Abolitionists treat the first as an unacceptable horror and the second as an acceptable cost of doing enlightened business. It is the opposite. A policy that spares the worst murderers and consigns their future victims to death in their place is not moral caution. It is moral cowardice dressed up as humanitarianism. The innocence argument, properly understood, does not weigh against capital punishment. It weighs decisively in its favor.

## Incapacitation: The Guarantee Only Execution Provides

All of this converges on a final, unanswerable point. Only an executed murderer is guaranteed never to kill again.

Incarcerated killers murder guards and fellow inmates. Some escape. Some are released through commutations, appellate reversals, or the drift of future policy—policies that, as Connecticut demonstrated with Hayes and Komisarjevsky, can reach back retroactively to undo sentences imposed after painstaking trials. Every living murderer represents an unresolved risk; every executed murderer represents certainty. For Richard Ramirez, for Dzhokhar Tsarnaev, for the men who tortured the Petit family to death, the promise that they will never again touch an innocent life is not a minor consideration. It is the state fulfilling its most fundamental duty: the protection of its citizens.

## Conclusion: End the Moral Failure

The case is complete. Proportionality establishes what justice requires. Deterrence shows that justice saves future lives. Prosecutorial leverage shows that justice resolves past crimes. Democratic legitimacy shows that the American people have repeatedly demanded this justice. The innocence myth collapses under examination and reverses against its proponents. Incapacitation closes the circle by guaranteeing that the worst offenders never kill again. These arguments do not stand alone; they reinforce each other, converging from law, economics, ethics, history, and democratic practice into a single conclusion that no serious person should be able to evade.

Abolitionism is not a higher morality. It is a moral failure. It is the position that tells the parents of murdered children that their loss does not warrant society's most serious response; that tells the families of the missing that the killer will take his secrets to a natural grave; that tells future victims of released murderers that their deaths are an acceptable price for protecting the lives of the men who will kill them; that tells the American people their repeated democratic judgments can be quietly overridden by litigation and supply-chain sabotage. It is not enlightenment. It is an abdication—of justice, of courage, of the basic duty a civilization owes the innocent.

The United States should reverse course. Every state should authorize capital punishment for the crimes that warrant it: serial murder, the murder of children, mass-casualty terrorism, murder committed by those already serving life, and the most calculated and sadistic killings. States that have abolished it should restore it. States that retain it should use it—not sparingly, not apologetically, not after decades of delay, but seriously, deliberately, and within reasonable time frames, in the cases where guilt is overwhelming and the crime is monstrous. The federal government should resume executions for federal capital offenses without apology.

Hayley Petit was seventeen. Michaela Petit was eleven. They died tied to their beds, in smoke and fire, because two men chose to put them there. The men who did it are alive. That is not justice. That is not mercy. That is not civilization. A country that cannot bring itself to execute the men who did that to those girls is a country that has lost its nerve, and a country that has lost its nerve cannot protect its children. America should recover the moral clarity its Constitution, its people, and its victims demand. Justice requires it. Decency requires it. The innocent—those already lost and those not yet born—require it.

Build the case that capital punishment is a just, effective, and necessary tool of American criminal justice.
transcript.subject_reply:
# Justice Demands the Ultimate Penalty: The Case for Capital Punishment in America

Some crimes are so monstrous that any punishment short of death fails the victims, the community, and the very idea of justice. When a terrorist detonates a bomb in a crowded square, when a killer tortures a child to death, when an assassin guns down a police officer in the line of duty — society is not merely inconvenienced. It is wounded. And a wound of that magnitude demands a response proportional to the evil inflicted. The death penalty, properly reserved for the worst of the worst, is not a relic of a cruder age. It is a moral, practical, and legal necessity that the United States should use actively and without apology.

## Justice Is Proportional, or It Is Not Justice

The foundational principle of any legitimate criminal justice system is proportionality: the punishment must fit the crime. We do not fine murderers the way we fine jaywalkers, because we recognize that the gravity of the offense must be mirrored in the gravity of the response. Yet when we refuse to execute even the most heinous killers — men who plotted for months to slaughter innocents, who tortured children, who massacred strangers in the name of ideology — we break this principle at its most important point.

Life in prison, for all its hardships, still permits the condemned to breathe, to read, to write letters, to watch the seasons pass through a window. The victims of Timothy McVeigh, Dylann Roof, and the Boston Marathon bombers will do none of these things. To deny their killers the ultimate sanction is to declare, implicitly, that what they did was not quite as bad as it was. Capital punishment is the state's solemn acknowledgment that some acts are so wicked that only the forfeiture of life answers them.

## Deterrence and Incapacitation Save Lives

Critics claim the death penalty does not deter. The evidence is more complicated than they admit. Studies by economists such as Hashem Dezhbakhsh, Paul Rubin, and Joanna Shepherd at Emory University have found that each execution is associated with multiple fewer homicides. Even setting aside contested statistical models, common sense and human experience tell us that people respond to incentives, and that the prospect of death is the most powerful incentive any legal system can deploy. A would-be cop-killer who knows he may face the needle has at least one more reason to lower his weapon.

But even without a single deterrent effect, capital punishment delivers absolute incapacitation. An executed murderer will never kill a guard, never order a hit from behind bars, never escape, never be paroled by a future commission, never murder again. Consider Kenneth McDuff, released from Texas death row after the 1972 Furman decision commuted his sentence — he went on to kill at least nine more women. Every such case is an indictment of a system too squeamish to finish the job.

## The Social Contract Demands It

A government's first duty is to protect its citizens, and certain crimes represent direct assaults on the institutions that make civilized life possible. The murder of a police officer is an attack on the rule of law itself. An act of terrorism is an attack on the civic peace the Constitution exists to preserve. The killing of a child is an attack on the most defenseless members of the community. When the state refuses to impose the ultimate penalty for these ultimate crimes, it signals weakness at precisely the moments it must signal resolve.

The death penalty is also an expression of democratic will. It has been repeatedly affirmed by Congress, by state legislatures, by juries of ordinary citizens, and by the Supreme Court. More than two-thirds of the states retained capital punishment on their books, and the federal government does as well. This is not the vestige of a dying consensus — it is the living judgment of the American people that some crimes must be answered with death.

## Closure for Victims and Dignity for the Dead

Too often, discussions of capital punishment fixate on the killer and ignore the families of the slain. These families live with holes that never close: birthdays missed, graduations unattended, grandchildren never met. For many, knowing that the person who destroyed their world is still eating, still exercising, still receiving mail and visitors, is an ongoing torment. Executions do not erase grief, but they offer something essential: the knowledge that the law took the crime seriously enough to answer it in kind.

There is dignity in this. To treat murder victims as though their lives warranted the maximum response the state can muster is to honor them. To shrug and hand their killer a cell is to diminish them.

## Modern Safeguards Have Addressed Past Flaws

The most common objection is the risk of executing the innocent. This concern deserves to be taken seriously — and it has been. Modern capital cases involve automatic appeals, mandatory state supreme court review, federal habeas review, DNA testing, and years, often decades, of scrutiny. The conditions under which executions occur today bear little resemblance to the rushed, evidence-thin prosecutions of earlier eras. Where doubt remains, the death penalty should not be imposed. But in cases of clear guilt — the terrorist caught on video, the mass shooter apprehended at the scene, the killer who confesses and leads police to the bodies — the epistemic argument collapses. We are not uncertain whether Dylann Roof murdered nine people in a Charleston church. We are not uncertain whether the Boston Marathon bombers placed those backpacks. For such cases, hesitation is not humility. It is abdication.

## Conclusion

A justice system that cannot bring itself to execute the worst offenders it prosecutes is a system that has lost faith in its own moral seriousness. The death penalty, used deliberately and reserved for the gravest crimes, affirms that human life is precious precisely by refusing to let those who destroy it walk away with their own lives intact. It protects the public, honors victims, upholds the social contract, and gives the law teeth commensurate with the evils it must confront. The United States should not merely retain capital punishment — it should use it, consistently and without apology, for premeditated murder, for terrorism, for the killing of children and of the officers who shield us from chaos. Anything less is a betrayal of the victims we claim to mourn and the justice we claim to serve.

Abolitionists often respond that murderers act on impulse and do not calculate. This is false as a generalization and irrelevant where it is true. These are precisely the offenders most susceptible to deterrence, and precisely the offenders the death penalty targets.

And even where deterrence is imperfect, incapacitation is absolute. An executed murderer will never kill a guard, never order a hit from behind bars, never escape, never be paroled by a future commission, never murder again. Consider Kenneth McDuff, released from Texas death row after the 1972 *Furman* decision commuted his sentence — he went on to kill at least nine more women. Every such case is an indictment of a system too squeamish to finish the job.

The deterrence argument takes on a special urgency inside prison walls, where a conveniently ignored truth becomes inescapable: without the death penalty, an inmate already serving life without parole faces no meaningful additional punishment for committing further murders. He can kill a correctional officer, stab a fellow inmate, orchestrate violence from his cell — and the legal system has nothing left to threaten him with. Another life sentence is a mathematical absurdity. Solitary confinement is an administrative choice, not a legal consequence. The deterrent structure of the criminal law simply ceases to function for these men. Only the prospect of execution restores it.

The case of Thomas Silverstein illustrates the stakes. Already serving multiple life sentences for murders inside the federal system, Silverstein murdered Corrections Officer Merle Clutts at the United States Penitentiary in Marion, Illinois, in 1983. Because the federal death penalty was effectively unavailable at the time, his punishment for killing a corrections officer was — more prison. He spent the rest of his life in extreme isolation, but he faced no legal consequence commensurate with having murdered a man whose only offense was showing up to work. Clutts's family received no justice. Every guard who walked a tier after 1983 knew that a lifer with a shank held all the leverage.

Silverstein is not an isolated example. Lifers and long-term inmates commit a disproportionate share of prison homicides. Correctional officers — men and women who accept low pay and high danger to keep the rest of us safe from people we prefer not to think about — are stabbed, beaten, and killed by offenders for whom the legal system has run out of threats. Fellow inmates, including those serving shorter sentences and trying to get home to their families, die at the hands of men who calculate, correctly, that they cannot be punished further.

The death penalty is the only tool that changes this calculus. A lifer who knows that murdering a guard can cost him his life has a reason to stay his hand. A lifer who knows it cannot has no reason at all. To abolish capital punishment is to tell every correctional officer in America that when they face the most dangerous prisoners in the system, the law will not stand behind them. It is to accept, as a matter of policy, a permanent class of untouchable killers living among captive victims. No civilized country should tolerate such a bargain.

## The Social Contract Demands It

A government's first duty is to protect its citizens, and certain crimes represent direct assaults on the institutions that make civilized life possible. The murder of a police officer is an attack on the rule of law itself. An act of terrorism is an attack on the civic peace the Constitution exists to preserve. The killing of a child is an attack on the most defenseless members of the community. When the state refuses to impose the ultimate penalty for these ultimate crimes, it signals weakness at precisely the moments it must signal resolve.

The death penalty is also an expression of democratic will. It has been repeatedly affirmed by Congress, by state legislatures, by juries of ordinary citizens, and by the Supreme Court. More than two-thirds of the states retained capital punishment on their books, and the federal government does as well. This is not the vestige of a dying consensus — it is the living judgment of the American people that some crimes must be answered with death.

## Closure for Victims and Dignity for the Dead

Too often, discussions of capital punishment fixate on the killer and ignore the families of the slain. These families live with holes that never close: birthdays missed, graduations unattended, grandchildren never met. For many, knowing that the person who destroyed their world is still eating, still exercising, still receiving mail and visitors, is an ongoing torment. Executions do not erase grief, but they offer something essential: the knowledge that the law took the crime seriously enough to answer it in kind.

There is dignity in this. To treat murder victims as though their lives warranted the maximum response the state can muster is to honor them. To shrug and hand their killer a cell is to diminish them.

## Modern Safeguards Have Addressed Past Flaws

The most common objection is the risk of executing the innocent. This concern deserves to be taken seriously — and it has been. Modern capital cases involve automatic appeals, mandatory state supreme court review, federal habeas review, DNA testing, and years, often decades, of scrutiny. The conditions under which executions occur today bear little resemblance to the rushed, evidence-thin prosecutions of earlier eras. Where doubt remains, the death penalty should not be imposed. But in cases of clear guilt — the terrorist caught on video, the mass shooter apprehended at the scene, the killer who confesses and leads police to the bodies — the epistemic argument collapses. We are not uncertain whether Dylann Roof murdered nine people in a Charleston church. We are not uncertain whether the Boston Marathon bombers placed those backpacks. For such cases, hesitation is not humility. It is abdication.

## Conclusion

A justice system that cannot bring itself to execute the worst offenders it prosecutes is a system that has lost faith in its own moral seriousness. The death penalty, used deliberately and reserved for the gravest crimes, affirms that human life is precious precisely by refusing to let those who destroy it walk away with their own lives intact. It protects the public from free killers and the incarcerated from captive ones, honors victims, upholds the social contract, and gives the law teeth commensurate with the evils it must confront. The United States should not merely retain capital punishment — it should use it, consistently and without apology, for premeditated murder, for terrorism, for the killing of children, for the murder of the officers who shield us from chaos, and for the killers who strike from within the walls of our prisons. Anything less is a betrayal of the victims we claim to mourn and the justice we claim to serve.
